text: This video describes two cases of invasive perianal Paget's disease treated with robotic abdominal perineal resection. The authors have no declarations.

The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg with the right side down. The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection of the left mesocolon, exposing, ligating, and dividing the IMA pedicle. A total mesorectal excision is carried out down to the pelvic floor, stopping proximal to the levators. The proximal sigmoid is divided with an endoscopic stapler, and an end colostomy is matured. The patient is repositioned in Kraski position for a wide local excision of the perineum, maintaining dissection in the extra levator plane. The posterior vaginal wall is excised en bloc, and intraoperative frozen sections from vaginal wall margins are sent for histopathology, all returning negative. The dissection includes macroscopically abnormal skin and deepens cephalad to connect with the abdominal dissection.
The surgeon reconstructs the posterior vaginal wall using interrupted 3-0 Vicryl sutures, followed by primary closure of the perineal defect.

The second case concerns a sixty-nine-year-old female with a past medical history of breast and endometrial cancer who underwent a colonoscopy. She was found to have anorectal adenocarcinoma with extensive perianal Paget's disease. Once again, a robotic extra-elevator, APR, with posterior vaginectomy was performed. Using a medial to lateral dissection, the IMA was exposed, ligated with clips and divided. A total mesorectal excision was performed to the level of the levators. Once again, the bowel was divided with an endoscopic stapler. The patient was placed into the jackknife Kraski position and a wide perineal clearance was undertaken to include the posterior vagina. In this case, the perineal defect was closed via a gluteal thigh flap, courtesy of the plastic surgeons.

The patient made an uneventful recovery and was discharged. In conclusion, perianal Paget's disease is an intractable neoplastic disorder. that can be associated with other malignant diseases of the anal canal and colorectum. Colonoscopy should therefore be performed during the preoperative workup. Wide local excision of the skin lesion with clear margins is sufficient when invasive disease is absent. Invasive disease requires an extra levator, APR. The method of peroneal reconstruction depends upon the size of the defect and individual patient anatomical characteristics. Where primary closure is not possible, a gluteal thigh flap can be used for reconstruction. This is particularly well suited for minimally invasive surgery as an abdominal dissection is not required.
